does: This paper presents incremental progress on mechanistic interpretability through two focused investigations. It examines successor heads—attention patterns that predict the next token in sequences—by analyzing their behavior across different model architectures and scales. Simultaneously, it explores oversampling techniques within Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs), investigating whether training SAEs with repeated or weighted data points improves feature discovery and reconstruction quality. The work combines empirical experiments on these topics, documenting findings about head specialization patterns and SAE training dynamics, contributing practical insights to the interpretability toolkit.
connects: This work builds on the circuits research program that seeks to decompose neural network behavior into interpretable computational units. It extends prior investigation into attention head specialization and function by zooming in on successor heads specifically—a natural continuation of work analyzing how language models predict sequential information. The SAE component connects to the growing literature on using autoencoders to discover sparse, interpretable features in neural activations, building directly on recent techniques for scaling SAEs and improving their reconstruction fidelity. Both investigations respond to fundamental challenges in mechanistic interpretability about identifying and isolating meaningful computational mechanisms.
enables: These targeted investigations support the broader goal of reverse-engineering language models by documenting which attention patterns and feature representations matter for prediction. Better understanding of successor heads could reveal how models encode sequential reasoning and planning. Improved SAE training methods through oversampling could lead to more faithful feature dictionaries, enabling more reliable causal interventions and ablations. By systematizing these mechanisms, the work creates foundations for higher-level interpretability research—understanding how multiple heads and features compose to solve complex reasoning tasks, and potentially identifying failure modes or biases encoded in learned circuits.
